content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      :   Members of the Commission                                Date  :  July 28, 1980


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Sarah Cameron [signature]

Subject:  Standardization of Conflict of Interest Codes for
         which the Commission is the code reviewing body


         One of the major findings of our Oversight Project was
that Conflict of Interest Codes were not being kept up-to-date
and in conformance with the Act and current Commission regula-
tions. My examination of the 391 Codes which the Commission
had approved when I wrote my May memorandum concerning standards
for exceptions from standardization showed that only 17 agencies,
slightly more than 4 percent, had ever amended their Codes.
Even if one looks only at those Codes adopted in 1978 or earlier,
on the theory that later Codes would not need as many amendments,
the total which have been amended is still less than 6 percent.
Primarily due to the inclusion of definitional material in
Codes, but also due in part to such provisions as the 10-day-
before assuming office statement, this failure to update Codes
means that there undoubtedly are substantive differences between
filers' obligations under their Codes and what they are in-
structed to do by our 730 Form and Manual. There can be no
doubt that we cannot rely upon many individual agencies to keep
their designated employees well informed concerning their obli-
gations under the Act; although there are a few which do a good
job, the vast majority have neither the expertise nor the in-
clination to do so. If we want to expand and perfect our tech-
nical assistance program to make more accurate information
readily accessible to filing officers as well as designated
employees, given our limited size and budget, we must achieve
as much standardization as we can.

         The process of standardization must take into account the
individual situation of each agency, however, and as I noted in
my May memo, I expect there to be Codes which must depart from
the standard Code, either to make sure that all foreseeable
conflicts of interest are avoided or to alleviate what would
otherwise be an overly burdensome disclosure obligation. The
open question is what to do about agencies who want to maintain
in their Codes provisions currently in them which differ sub-
stantively from the standard Code, but which do not achieve one
of these two purposes. Before answering this question, you
wanted to know how many Codes fall into this category.

Members of the Commission
July 28, 1980
Page Two


As we have proceeded along the course of standardization,
we have tried to obtain as much participation as we could
from the agencies which would be affected by it. We sent a
memorandum concerning standardization to all agencies for
which we are the code reviewing body as well as to all cities
and counties. Although we received comments from several
cities and counties and from a few attorneys who represent
local special districts, the only agencies for which we are
the code reviewing body which responded were a few water
agencies and the University of California. The concerns of
the water agencies, Metropolitan Water District of Southern
California, Westlands Water District and the East Bay Municipal
Utility District (which has other functions in addition to
water utility), along with the concerns of the Association
of California Water Agencies (ACWA), have been dealt with at
length before and may well be raised again when the agencies
come before us individually. With respect to agencies which
did not respond to our request for comments on the standardi­
zation process, the following may be some indication of
where controversy might arise.

First, in order to find Codes in which there were any
issues of controversy without reading through all 391 files,
I reviewed the minutes of all Commission and Committee meetings.
The two criteria I used to identify potentially controversial
Codes were (1) whether someone other than a single representa­
tive of the agency testified at either of the hearings, and
(2) whether the Code was put over from meeting to meeting.
This process yielded 34 agencies, three of which were the
aforementioned water agencies and one of which was UC. Then
Jeanne Pritchard, Dwight Dickerson and I reviewed the files
of all of these agencies to determine from the staff memo­
randa and correspondence what the areas of controversy in
the Codes were.

With respect to 17 of these Codes, either the controversy
was limited to the designations and disclosure categories,
or the Code has since been amended to conform to a standard
model. Three of the Codes have unusual provisions in the
body of the Code which could be dealt with instead in the
disclosure categories without any substantive difference.
Seven of the Codes have special provisions which establish
different definitions from those generally used in the Act.
One of these seven establishes a narrower definition than
that in the Act itself and this provision could probably be
put in the disclosure categories instead of in the body of
the Code; the rest of these Codes could have exceptions
along the lines of the Corporations sample which is attached.
The remaining agencies consist of the three aforementioned
water agencies and four others.

Members of the Commission
July 28, 1980
Page Three


Therefore, even assuming that there are a few Codes
which contain unusual provisions which were the subject of
some controversy and compromise, but which were not picked
up by this survey, it appears that there will only be a
limited number of agencies which will be able to point to a
legitimate substantial difference between the Codes they
currently have in effect and the standard Code contained in
Regulation 18730. Our experience with CSAC and the League
of Cities shows that an initial reaction opposing standardization can be overcome by pointing out the benefits in ease
of administration and accuracy of technical assistance. I
therefore do not expect a large number of strenuous requests
for exceptions from the standard Code, and for the reasons
stated in the May memorandum, including the failure of most
agencies to monitor and update their Codes, I still recommend
that the Commission require any agency which wants an exception
to justify its request.

STC:kp

CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE FOR

THE DEPARTMENT OF CORPORATIONS
[SAMPLE]


The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections

81000, et seq., requires state and local government agencies

to adopt and promulgate Conflict of Interest Codes. The

Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation,

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, which contains the terms of

a standard Conflict of Interest Code, which can be incorporated

by reference, and which may be amended by the Fair Political

Practices Commission to conform to amendments in the Political

Reform Act after public notice and hearings. Therefore, the

terms of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730 and any amendments

to it duly adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission,

along with the attached Appendix in which officials and

employees are designated and disclosure categories are set

forth, are hereby incorporated by reference and constitute

the Conflict of Interest Code of the Department of Corporations,

except as provided below.

     Pursuant to Section 4(A) of the standard Code,

designated employees shall file statements of economic interests

with their agencies. Upon receipt of the statement of the

Commissioner, the agency shall make and retain a copy and

forward the original of this statement to the Fair Political

Practices Commission.

     Exceptions: As provided in Section 1 of the standard

Code, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730(b)(1), the definitions

contained in the Political Reform Act of 1974 shall apply to

the terms used in this Code except that the term "business

entity" shall apply to any organization or enterprise whether

or not operated for profit; the term "investment" shall

apply not only to business entities which own real property

in the jurisdiction, do business or plan to do business in

the jurisdiction or have done business in the jurisdiction

at any time during the two years prior to the time any statement

or other action is required under this Code, but also to any

business entity which has applied for or received a permit,

registration, license, certificate, order or opinion from

the Commissioner at any time during the two years prior to

the time any statement or other action is required under

this Code; and the term "investment" shall include a time or

demand deposit in any financial institution or credit union

subject to the jurisdiction of the Commissioner.

- 2 -

sections.question_synthetic: This is an internal FPPC memorandum to Commission members regarding the standardization of Conflict of Interest Codes, not a response to an external legal question.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal memorandum to Commission members, not an advice letter responding to an external request. Contains no question/conclusion sections typical of advice letters. Includes a sample standardized Conflict of Interest Code as attachment. Document discusses oversight findings and recommendations regarding code standardization.

embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum from Sarah Cameron dated July 28, 1980, discussing standardization of Conflict of Interest Codes across 391 agencies for which the Commission is the reviewing body, with findings that most codes have not been updated and a sample standardized code for the Department of Corporations included.
